coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous electrodialytic recovery of HCl and LiOH from LiCl: Modeling, optimization and experimental validation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cerrillo-Gonzalez, Maria del Mar
Casaburo, Raffaele
Luciani, Giuseppina
Paz-Garcia, Juan Manuel
Rodriguez-Maroto, Jose Miguel
Villen-Guzman, Maria
coredata.dc:description: The growing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) highlights the need for sustainable recycling technologies. Electro-electrodialysis (EED) has emerged as a promising alternative for regenerating chemical reagents and recovering lithium-based salts from hydrometallurgical leachates. This study investigates the electrodialytic production of HCl and LiOH from LiCl solutions using a four compartment EED cell. A mathematical model was developed to describe ions transports, acid and base regeneration and energy consumption, incorporating electrochemical and mass transport parameters. The model was validated through five batch-mode EED experiments under different operating conditions. Experimental results validated the model's accuracy in predicting the concentrations of Li+, Cl−, H+ and OH− over time, as well as the voltage and specific energy consumption, with R2 values above 0.99. The optimal performance was achieved under conditions of high initial LiOH concentration (0.5 M) and low electrode distance (8 cm), which minimized the energy consumption while maintaining high product yields. Conversely, higher initial acid concentrations reduced ion recovery, likely due to increased ionic competition and membrane transport resistance. Under the optimal conditions, specific energy consumption reached value 8.3 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 13.7 kWh·kg−1 LiOH. The validated model provides a reliable tool for optimizing EED performance and reducing experimental effort. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using EED to selectively generate acid and base from LiCl-rich solutions and supports the development of more efficient approaches for LIB recycling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery recycling
Hydrometallurgy
Electrodialysis
Acid and base production
Close-loop recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preliminary study using the mathemathical model
Influence of initial acid and base concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of a closed-loop hydrometallurgical recycling process for spent LIBs.
Scheme of the batch-mode electrodialysis experimental setup.

Effect of the applied current on the concentration evolution in the (a) acid compartment, (b) salt compartment and (c) base compartment (Conditions: Initial concentration 0.2 M HCl │ 0.8 M LiCl │0.3 M LiOH).

Effect of the EED cell length (distance between the electrodes) on the ionic resistance evolution in the (a) acid compartment, (b) salt compartment and (c) base compartment (Conditions: Initial concentration 0.2 M HCl │ 0.8 M LiCl │0.3 M LiOH; 2.5 A).
Effect of the initial LiCl concentration of the ion recovery and SEC of a) Cl— and b) Li+ (Conditions: 0.2 M HCl │ 0.25–2 M LiCl │0.3 M LiOH; 2.5 A, 3 h).

Electrical resistance evolution over time in each compartment at different initial HCl and LiOH concentration: (a) 0.05 M HCl in acid compartment and 0.05 M LiOH in base compartment; (b) 0.5 M HCl in acid compartment and 0.5 M LiOH in base compartment. (Conditions: 2.5 A; 0.8 M LiCl).
Ionic resistance evolution over time as a function of a) initial HCl concentration in the acid compartment and b) initial LiOH concentration in the base compartment.

Effect of length cell (ED1 = 16 cm and ED4 = 8 cm) on the concentration of (a) acid compartment, (b) feed compartment, (c) base compartment and (d) cell voltage. (Conditions: 2.5 A; 0.2 M HCl│ 0.8 M LiCl│0.5 M LiOH).
Energy consumption (kWh·kg−1) and current efficiency (%) of (a) HCl production and (b) LiOH production at different distances of the electrode.

Concentration evolution in the acid, base and feed compartment during the electrodialysis test at different initial acid and base concentration. a) 0.2 M HCl│0.3 M LiOH; b) 0.4 M HCl│0.3 M LiOH; c) 0.2 M HCl │0.5 M LiOH; d) 0.4 M HCl│0.5 M LiOH (Conditions: 2.5 A; 0.8 M LiCl).
Energy consumption (kWh·kg−1) and current efficiency (%) of (a) HCl production and (b) LiOH production at different initial acid and base solution concentration.

Experimental and model results for: (a) the final concentration in acid (triangles), salt (diamonds) and base (circles) compartments; (b) ion recovery ratio of Cl− (triangles) and Li+ (circles); and (c) specific energy consumption (kWh/kg) for HCl (triangles) and LiOH (circles).
Mass balance equations for each species in the different compartment.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous electrodialytic recovery of HCl and LiOH from LiCl: Modeling, optimization and experimental validation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga. Malaga, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga. Malaga, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical, Materials and Production Engineering, University of Naples Federico II, Naples, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical, Materials and Production Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical, Materials and Production Engineering, University of Naples Federico II, Naples, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) highlights the need for sustainable recycling technologies. Electro-electrodialysis (EED) has emerged as a promising alternative for regenerating chemical reagents and recovering lithium-based salts from hydrometallurgical leachates. This study investigates the electrodialytic production of HCl and LiOH from LiCl solutions using a four compartment EED cell. A mathematical model was developed to describe ions transports, acid and base regeneration and energy consumption, incorporating electrochemical and mass transport parameters. The model was validated through five batch-mode EED experiments under different operating conditions. Experimental results validated the model's accuracy in predicting the concentrations of Li+, Cl−, H+ and OH− over time, as well as the voltage and specific energy consumption, with R2 values above 0.99. The optimal performance was achieved under conditions of high initial LiOH concentration (0.5 M) and low electrode distance (8 cm), which minimized the energy consumption while maintaining high product yields. Conversely, higher initial acid concentrations reduced ion recovery, likely due to increased ionic competition and membrane transport resistance. Under the optimal conditions, specific energy consumption reached value 8.3 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 13.7 kWh·kg−1 LiOH. The validated model provides a reliable tool for optimizing EED performance and reducing experimental effort. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using EED to selectively generate acid and base from LiCl-rich solutions and supports the development of more efficient approaches for LIB recycling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are crucial in powering many modern technologies, including portable electronic devices, electric vehicles (EVs), and renewable energy storage systems. The global demand for LIBs will increase significantly in the following decades due to the transition to cleaner energy and electric mobility policies [1]. However, this growing demand has associated several challenges related to the environmental impact of LIB manufacturing and the management of batteries at the end of their life. On the one hand, LIBs contain metals, such as cobalt and nickel, in addition to organic solvents that can threaten the ecosystems and human health if they enter the environment [2]. On the other hand, LIB manufacturing relies on raw materials, such as lithium, cobalt, and nickel, which are classified as critical by the European Commission due to their high economic importance and high supply risk [3]. A shift towards a circular economy is required to address these challenges and make LIBs a true enabler of the green transition [4]. This model emphasizes the extension of battery life, the design of products to be reused, and the recycling of battery wastes, focusing on the recovery of valuable components. Hence, end-of-life LIBs could be a secondary source of critical materials [5].

Currently, LIB recycling processes are dominated by pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical methods [6]. Pyrometallurgy involves the use of high temperatures to recover metals, while hydrometallurgy relies on acid leaching to dissolve metals from battery components. Although these methods are effective, they have notable drawbacks. Pyrometallurgy is energy-intensive and releases harmful emissions, while hydrometallurgy requires large quantities of chemical reagent and water and produces significant amounts of wastewater [7]. Therefore, it is essential to develop more sustainable recycling technologies that minimize resource consumption and environmental impact.

One promising solution to enhance the sustainability of LIB recycling is the integration of electrodialysis (ED) into the hydrometallurgical process [8]. ED is a membrane-based separation technology which uses an electric field to move ions through selective ion-exchange membranes. This technique is commonly used in water desalination, but it has recently gained attention for its potential application in chemical recovery processes [9]. When ED is coupled with water electrolysis at the electrodes, the process is referred to as electro-electrodialysis (EED). The H+ and OH− ions, produced in the anode and cathode respectively, migrate and react with the system to form acids and bases. This hybrid approach has demonstrated significant potential for acid and base regeneration from salts solutions, while maintaining the ion selective of conventional ED systems [10]. In the context of LIB recycling, ED and EED offers the ability to selectively recover valuable metals from leachate solutions while regenerating essential chemicals for the hydrometallurgical, as acid and base solution. The acids and base regenerated from Li-salt solution can be directly reused in the leaching and precipitation stages, respectively. As represented in Fig. 1 , this integration establishes a close-loop hydrometallurgical scheme that minimize the chemical reagents consumption and the generation of hazardous waste [11,12].

In recent years, several researchers have investigated the feasibility of the ED application to produce both acid and base from lithium-based aqueous salt solutions. Kang et al. [13] investigated the electrodialysis of Li2SO4 solutions using a three-compartment membrane system to produce LiOH and H2SO4. Their system achieved a high ion recovery ratio for Li+ and SO4 2− (94.3 % and 87.5 %, respectively) under a current density of 833 A·m−2. The study examined the influence of current density, solution volumes, and initial concentrations, revealing that increasing solution volume enhanced ion recovery and current efficiency. Chen et al. [14] evaluated the use of ED with bipolar membranes (BMED) to also produced LiOH and H2SO4 from pre-purified Li2SO4. The study demonstrated the feasibility of producing 2.2 M LiOH and 1.3 M H2SO4 from a 1.5 M Li2SO4 feed solution, with a specific energy consumption of less than 10 kWh·kg−1 of LiOH. Moreover, the process was optimized by minimizing sulphate ion migration through the bipolar membrane, achieving a LiOH purity of 99.75 % and lowering energy consumption to 7 kWh·kg−1. Bipolar membranes were also employed by Zhu et al. [15] to produce LiOH and H2SO4. The study explores the effects of current density, number of cell pairs and flow rates on overall production efficiency, being the number of cell pairs the main parameter to improve the cell performance and reduce energy consumption. Xiang et al. [16] explored the electrodialysis technique to treat high-salinity waste generated after recovery step using NaOH and Na2CO3 to precipitate metals including Li. The study showed that ED, following an oxidative pretreatment, successfully converted Na2SO4 solution into H2SO4 and NaOH, achieving current efficiencies of 52.53 % and 62.12 %, respectively. Yan et al. [17] proposed a hybrid bipolar membrane selective electrodialysis (BMSED) process for the simultaneous recovery of LiOH and cobalt salt from citric acid-leaching solutions of spent lithium-ion batteries, achieving a LiOH purity of 99.96 % and highlighting the potential of BMSED for integrated metal recovery and acid/base regeneration. In addition to these approaches, electro-metathesis membrane reactors have also been recently proposed as an alternative configuration for the simultaneous production of acids and bases from salt solutions. These systems rely on specific ion exchange and metathesis reactions facilitated by tailored membrane arrangements, offering promising performance for integrated chemical recovery [18].

Most studies published on the application of ED-based processes to produce acid and base from lithium salt solution are focused on experimental work, reporting results obtained under different operating conditions, feed compositions and cell configuration. These studies are essential to understand the fundamentals of the technique and to evaluate its potential as a separation process. However, there is still a lack of theoretical studies supported by mathematical models [19,20]. It must be noted that the development of models is not only important to predict the experimental result but also to improve the mechanism involved in the ion transport. Moreover, mathematical models are valuable tools to identify the key parameters affecting ED performance and to support the design and optimization of the process [20,21].

Although a wide range of models have been developed to describe ions transport in ED systems [22–25], these models have been mainly focused on the dilution and concentration of salts, especially for desalination processes using NaCl solutions [24]. In this context, recent research has begun to address the gap in theoretical modeling for lithium salt systems. Asadi et al. [19] developed and validated a comprehensive mathematical model for ED separation of LiOH and H2SO4 from Li2SO4 solution. The model was based on the Nerst-Plank equation, and incorporate mass and ion conservation, concentration polarization and electro-osmotic water transport.

Despite the growing interest in ED for lithium recovery, most studies have been focused on lithium sulfate (Li2SO4) solution, while other lithium salts have been less investigated. The selection of Li2SO4 is motivated by the fact that H2SO4 is the most used acid in the leaching process of LIBs battery [4]. After valuable metals such as Co, Mn or Ni are recovered by precipitation using LiOH, the final solution is mainly composed by Li2SO4 due to the difficulty of precipitating lithium ions. However, other leaching agents, such as HCl and HNO3, are also used to dissolve the metals contained in the LIBs black mass [26]. In these alternative routes, where HCl is used for leaching and LiOH for metal precipitation, the final solution is mainly composed by LiCl [27,28]. Compared to the conventional Li₂SO₄ route, the use of LiCl offers some advantages. LiCl electrodialysis regenerates both HCl and LiOH, enabling a closed-loop operation with lower external reagent demand, while eliminating sulfate impurities that often limit LiOH purity in Li₂SO₄ systems.

In this work, an alternative electro-electrodialysis (EED) system is proposed for the simultaneous recovery of HCl and LiOH from LiCl solutions, using a simple four-compartment configuration equipped with conventional AEM and CEM. Unlike other ED-based processes, such as BMED or electro-metathesis membrane reactors which typically require complex membrane assemblies or intermediate chemical steps, this configuration enables the direct acid and base production from a single salt feed without the need for external reagents. To support process optimization, a mathematical model is developed to describe ion transports, predict ions concentration profiles in the EED cell and evaluate the effect of the different operating parameters on system performance. The model is validated experimentally under different operating conditions. This combined experimental-theoretical approach contributed to the development of efficient, reagent-free strategies for acid and base regeneration within circular hydrometallurgical processes.

A mathematical model was developed to simulate an electrodialysis process designed to produce HCl and LiOH from LiCl salt solution. The modelled system, schematically presented in Fig. 2 , consists of a four compartment electrodialytic cell: anolyte compartment (Comp. 0), acid (Comp. 1) salt compartment (Comp. 2) and the base compartment (Comp. 3). The compartments are separated by two cation exchange membranes (CEM) and one anion exchange membranes (AEM) arranged in the following sequence: Anode - Anolyte - CEM - Acid compartment -AEM - Salt compartment - CEM - Base compartment - Cathode. The anolyte compartment between the anode and the first CEM serves a crucial safety function, preventing the formation of chlorine gas on the anode surface.

When an electric current is applied, the ionic species in the electrodialysis cell migrate to other compartments driven by the electric field applied between the electrodes. In the salt compartment, containing the LiCl solution, Li+ cations migrate towards the cathode, passing through the CEM into the base compartment. In this compartment, OH− are generated via water reduction at the cathode surface and subsequently combine with Li+ to form LiOH. Simultaneously, Cl− anions migrate towards the anode, crossing the AEM into the acid compartment. There, protons produced by water oxidation in the anode and transported from the anolyte to the acid compartment, react with Cl− to generate HCl. This phenomenon of ions migration and electrochemical reactions defines the mechanism of salt splitting, which was the basis for the development of the mathematical model. Therefore, the model was based on fundamental principles of mass-transfer within the compartments and ion migration across the membranes. To simplify the analysis, the following assumptions were considered:
1.
The electrodialysis system operates in a batch configuration, in which all solution streams (acid, salt, and base) are continuously recirculated until the target time or concentration is reached. Complete mixing is assumed in each channel, and turbulent flow conditions are maintained to ensure uniformity.
2.
The applied current and temperature is kept constant during the ED operation.
3.
The effects of pressure on ion transport, water flux and Donnan equilibrium approach are assumed to be negligible.
4.
The ions concentration of each channel solutions (acid, salt and base) changes over time due to the ion transport through the membranes under the effect of the applied current. This variation can be determined through a mass balance of ions between the compartments. Fig. 2 details the ions transportation inside the cell. As shown, ions are transported from the salt (dilute) channel to the acid or base (concentrate) channel, resulting in an increase in concentration in the concentrate channel and a decrease in the dilute channel. Therefore, the mass balance equations for cations and anions can be described by the Eq. (1):
(1)

C

dt
=

C
i , n
(mol m−3) is the concentration of the ion i in the compartment x (1: acid; 2: salt; 3: base); I (A) is the applied current; F (96,485C mol−1) is the Faraday constant; z
i (−) is the ion charge; V (m3) is the solution volume; and
t
i , x

i ,
x + 1
is the ion i transport number through the membranes surrounded the compartment n (i.e

= left membrane,

= right membrane).
5.
The transport number quantifies the fraction of the electric current carried out by each species across the membrane and can be evaluated as:
(2)

6.
The permeability coefficient,
f i
, evaluates the ions rate moving across the membranes. It is dimensionless and assumes values from 0 to 1, where zero means complete impermeability to an ion species. In this model, for cation exchange membranes (CEM),
f i
was 0 and 1 for anion and cations, respectively, while for anion exchange membranes (AEM),
f i
was assumed 1 for anion and 0 for cations, except for protons. In this model,
f i
for protons in AEM was defined using the following correlation based on the proton concentration:
(3)

where C

+ (mol m−3) represents the proton concentration in the acid compartment and a and b are coefficients determined from the experimental data. This approach is supported by previous studies [20,25], which experimentally confirmed the significant protons leakage across AEMs at high acid concentration.
7.
Considering the mass balance equation for ions (Eq.(1)), the transport ion number (Eq.(2)) and the permeability coefficient, the mass balance equation for each species in each compartment is detailed in Table 1
.
8.
The model includes the water self-ionization reaction which is assumed to be in equilibrium
(4)

9.
The electric resistance of each compartment, R

(5)

=

λ i
where L (cm) is the width of the compartment and S (cm2) is the membrane surface.

To validate the proposed model and estimate the model parameters, a series of experimental tests were conducted. A schematic diagram of the experimental system is presented in Fig. 3 . The EED cell was built using four methacrylate compartments, separated by two CEMs (Neosepta CSE) and one AEM (Neosepta ASE), arranged alternatively between the two electrodes. The effective area for each membrane was 50 cm2, and the space between these membranes was 4 cm in ED1 and 2 cm in ED2-ED5. The compartment between the anode and the CEM serves a crucial safety function, preventing the formation of chlorine gas on the anode surface. For this reason, 0.1 M HNO3 (HNO3, 65 %, analytical grade, Panreac) was selected as the anolyte solution. Deionized water and different initial concentrations of LiOH (0.3 M and 0.5 M) and HCl (0.2 M and 0.4 M) were used in the base and acid compartment (i.e. concentrate solution), respectively (LiOH·H2O, 98 %, analytical grade, Sigma-Aldrich; HCl, 35 %, analytical grade, Panreac). LiCl salt (LiCl, 99 %, analytical grade, Sigma-Aldrich) solution was used as the feed/salt solution (i.e. dilute solution), with a fixed concentration of 0.8 M in all the experiments. The selected LiCl concentration was chosen as a representative intermediate value based on literature data for lithium-rich leachate solutions obtained after precipitation of other metals [28–31].

To ensure homogenous mixing of all solutions, each compartment was connected to an external container, where the solution was continuously mixed by a magnetic stirrer. In all experiments, the initial volume of the external solutions (salt, acid and base) was 0.4 L. A four channel peristaltic pump (Masterflex®) was used to recirculate each solution between the external containers and their respective channel. The flow rate of solutions was kept constant at 3 cm3·s−1 in all the experiments. It must be noted that the EED cell operated in a batch configuration. Prior to the experiment, all solutions flow through the compartments for 10 min to eliminate the visible gas bubbles inside the EED cell. All the experiments were performed at constant current of 2.5 A (i.e. 50 mA cm−2 referred to the internal diameter of the membrane), employing DC power supply (Genesys RDK Lambda GEN 600–2.6). Details for each experiment are collected in Table 2
.

Total experimental time was fixed at 3 h, as this time was sufficient to achieve nearly complete transportation of Li+ and Cl− ions from feed compartment to base and acid compartments, under the selected current and initial LiCl concentration. Samples of 10 mL were collected at different times to determine the ions concentration. Lithium was analyzed via Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry (Agilent 55AA). H+ and OH− concentration was determined by acid-base titration (Phenolphthalein, 0.1 M NaOH and 0.2 M HCl). The Mohr method was employed for the chloride ions analysis (K2CRO4, 0.01 M AgNO3). Moreover, the mass of each solution was weight before taking the samples to evaluate the change of solution volume during the experiment due to the water osmosis.

The performance of EED system for HCl and LiOH production was evaluated by the specific energy consumption (SEC) and the current efficiency (CE), referred to the amount of HCl or LiOH produced. The specific energy consumption, SEC
i (kWh·kg−1 i produced) was calculated by Eq.(6) (adapted from [14]).
(6)
S E

where U (V) is the voltage drop across the EED cell; I (A) is the constant current applied; t (h) is the total time of experiment; C
i,0 and C
i,t (mol L−1) are the acid or base solution concentration at initial time and time t, respectively; V
i,0 and V
i,t (L) are the solution volume at initial time and time t; and M (g·mol−1) is the molar mass of HCl or LiOH.

The current efficiency, CE (%) can be determined as follows ([15]):
(7)

where z
i is the valence of ion i; F is the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1), and N is the number of cell units (i.e. N = 1).

Other parameters used to evaluate the efficiency of the EED system were the ion separation ratio (β) and the ion recovery ratio (γ). The ion separation ratio, defined as the quotient between the number of ions removed from the dilute compartment related to the initial, is calculated by the following eq. (8) (as reported in [13]):
(8)

Similarly, the ion recovery ratio is defined as the quotient between the increase of ions in the acid or base compartment referred to the initial content of the feed solution. The expression used to determine the ion recovery ratio of Li+ and Cl− in the base and acid compartment respectively is [15]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary study using the mathemathical model
ce:section.ce:para: A preliminary analysis was carried out using the developed mathematical model to evaluate the performance of the electrodialysis system for producing HCl and LiOH, simultaneously, from a LiCl solution. The aim was to examine how key operating parameters affect the process and to identify the most suitable range of operating conditions. Specifically, the effects of the applied current, the length of the electrodialytic compartments and the initial concentration of LiCl, HCl and LiOH were examined. These parameters were selected due to their direct impact on energy consumption and current efficiency.

Fig. 4 shows the concentration profile of HCl, LiCl and LiOH in their respective compartment for different applied currents, ranging from 0.25 to 3.0 A. In all cases, the initial concentration of HCl, LiCl and LiOH were fixed at 0.2 M, 0.8 M and 0.3 M, respectively. As observed in Fig. 4a and Fig. 4c, increasing the applied current leads to a faster accumulation of HCl in the acid compartment and LiOH in the base compartment. This behavior is consistent with the enhanced transport of chloride ions through AEM and lithium ions through CEM under stronger electric fields. It can be observed that in both cases, the concentration increases linearly for lower currents, while nonlinear behavior emerges at high current intensities due to concentration polarization effects and depletion in the adjacent compartments [32].

In the salt compartment (Fig. 4b), a gradual depletion of LiCl is observed, being more pronounced as the current increases. For currents below 1.0 A, the depletion is relatively low, but it becomes significant above 2.0 A, indicating the potential for complete salt depletion if the process is extended for longer times or operated at high current densities. These results highlight the critical role of the applied current in controlling the rate of product formation and salt splitting. While higher current enhances productivity, it also led to an increase of energy consumption and sharper concentration gradients, which could affect membrane performance and system stability.

Another parameter influencing energy consumption is the ionic resistance of the solution, which directly affects the voltage required to maintain a constant applied current. The ionic resistance depends on two primary factors: the length of each compartment and the ionic concentration of the solution. As described in Eq. (5), the ionic resistance, R
x
, is proportional to the compartment length (L) and inversely proportional to the sum of the products of the ionic concentrations (C
i,t ) and their respective molar conductivities (
λ i
). Consequently, for a fixed membrane surface area (S), both high length compartment and low ion concentration results in higher resistance values, increasing the energy demands of the system.

Fig. 5 shows the evolution of the ionic resistance over time in each compartment for different EED cell length (from 2 to 16 cm). As expected, increasing the EED cell length led to higher initial resistances values. Regarding the ionic resistance evolution, both acid and base compartment

Fig. 5a and Fig. 5c shows a decreasing trend over time for all tested lengths. This behavior is related to the continuous accumulation of HCl (acid compartment) and LiOH (base compartment), which increases the total ionic concentration and enhances the conductivity of the solution. In contrast, the salt compartment (Fig. 5b) shows an increasing resistance over time, due to the depletion of LiCl.

In addition to the EED cell length, the initial concentration of LiCl in the feed compartment also determines the electrodialysis performance, as directly affects both ionic resistance and the driving force for Cl— and Li+ transport. Lithium concentrations reported in leachates after precipitation typically range between 0.25 and 2 M [28–31]. To explore this variability, the model was used to simulate the influence of different initial LiCl concentrations, fixing the applied current at 2.5 A, the operation time at 3 h and the initial acid and base concentration at 0.2 M HCl and 0.3 M LiOH.

Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6 b shows the Cl— and Li+ recovery and the specific energy consumption (SEC) as a function of the initial LiCl concentration. At low concentration (0.25 M), the SEC drastically increases above 45 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 68 kWh·kg−1 LiOH due to the high resistance developed in the feed compartment as ions are rapidly depleted during the fixed operation time. Conversely, as the initial LiCl concentration increases, the SEC decreases and stabilizes around 10 kWh·kg−1 HCl and LiOH, while the recovery of Cl— and Li+ ions progressively declines.

These results highlight that a trade-off must be considered between maximizing ion recovery and minimizing energy consumption. In this context, the 0.8 M LiCl represents an intermediate condition that ensures acceptable Cl— and Li+ recovery while maintaining relatively low SEC. For this reason, this concentration was selected as the reference value in the experimental study.

After selecting 0.8 M LiCl as the initial feed concentration, the influence of the initial concentrations of HCl and LiOH on the performance of the electrodialysis cell was investigated. Fig. 7 compares the resistance evolution in each compartment (anolyte, acid, feed and base) under two different initial concentrations of HCl and LiOH. The anolyte resistance remains constant in both cases, while the feed compartment shows a gradual increase due to LiCl depletion. In Fig. 7a (0.05 M HCl and LiOH), the acid and base compartments show high initial resistance, and a decrease over time, while in Fig. 7b (0.5 M HCl and LiOH), resistance remains low and essentially stable during the process. These differences in the resistance behavior show the direct impact of the initial acid and base concentration on the overall energy efficiency of the EED process. It must be noted that lower concentrations in these compartments results in higher ionic resistance, while higher concentrations decrease the resistance but increase reagent consumption. Therefore, it is necessary to define a suitable operational range that balances electrical efficiency and chemical consumption.

To select the operational range of concentrations, the ionic resistance was further evaluated varying the initial concentrations of HCl and LiOH. Fig. 8 shows the influence of the initial reagent concentration on ionic resistance in both acid and base compartments. In the acid compartment (Fig. 8a), higher initial HCl concentrations significantly reduce the initial resistance, from 1.8 Ω at 0.05 M to below 0.3 Ω at 0.5 M. However, all conditions converge to similar ionic resistance levels at the end of the experiment. This trend indicates that the initial concentration primarily impacts the early stage of the operation. Based on these results and the reduced difference beyond 0.4 M HCl, an optimal HCl concentration range of 0.2–0.4 M was defined. A similar behavior is observed in the base compartment (Fig. 8b). Increasing the initial LiOH concentration from 0.05 M to 0.6 M significantly decreases the initial resistance, while the final values remain similar in all cases. Therefore, a suitable operational range of 0.3–0.5 M was selected for LiOH to ensure efficient performance without excessive reagent consumption.

To validate the model, a series of experiments were carried out under controlled operating conditions. These experiments aimed to compare the simulated results with experimental data and determine the accuracy of the model in predicting the concentration profiles and the performance of the electrodialysis process.

The effect of the electrode distance on the electrodialytic cell performance was studied. For that, two experiments were carried out, varying the length of the cell from 16 cm (ED1) to 8 cm (ED4). In both cases, the current was set at 2.5 A, and the initial concentration was 0.2 M HCl, 0.8 M LiCl and 0.5 M LiOH for acid, feed and base compartment, respectively. Fig. 9 shows the evolution of (a) HCl, (b) LiCl, and (c) LiOH and the (d) voltage profile.

Reducing the distance between electrodes significantly improves energy efficiency without affecting ion transport or product yield. Therefore, operating the electrodialysis cell with a shorter compartment length is favourable for minimizing energy consumption while maintaining high performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of initial acid and base concentration
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experiments were carried out to study the influence of the initial acid and base solution concentration on the electrodialysis cell performance. Four tests (ED2-ED5) were conducted varying the initial values of HCl (0.2 and 0.4 M) and LiOH (0.3 and 0.5 M), while the LiCl feed was fixed at 0.8 M and the current at 2.5 A. The evolution of HCl, LiCl and LiOH concentration and the feed solution pH during the experiment is represented in Fig. 11 .

From the results, it can be observed that the LiCl concentration in the feed compartment decreases over time, with longer operation times resulting in lower concentrations. In all cases, the final concentration approached approximately 0.2 M LiCl. However, the desalination rate was slower for the highest initial HCl concentration. In tests ED2 (Fig. 11a) and ED4 (Fig. 11c), both conducted with an initial HCl concentration of 0.2 M, the final LiCl concentration was 0.21 ± 0.01 M. Conversely, in tests ED3 (Fig. 11b) and ED5 (Fig. 11d), where the initial HCl concentration was 0.4 M, the final LiCl concentration was 0.27 ± 0.01 M. This difference can be attributed to the higher initial HCl concentration, which could hinder the electro-diffusion of chloride ions from the feed compartment to the acid compartment [16].

The Cl− and Li+ removed from the feed compartment and electro-diffused to their respective concentrate compartments led to an increase in the HCl and LiOH concentration. It can be observed from the results that in both concentrate compartments, higher initial concentration resulted in higher final concentration. In test ED3 and ED5 (initial 0.4 M HCl), the final HCl concentration was 0.85 M and 0.92 M, while in ED2 and ED4 (initial 0.2 M HCl) was 0.8 M and 0.78 M, respectively. A similar trend was observed in the base compartment. The final LiOH concentration in tests ED4 and ED5, both with an initial concentration of 0.5 M LiOH, was 0.98 M and 1.06 M, respectively. Whereas for ED2 and ED3 (initial 0.3 M LiOH), the final LiOH concentration was 0.79 M and 0.89 M.

However, higher initial acid and base concentration not only results in a greater final concentration in their respective compartments but also impacts on the overall ED system performance. Specifically, both separation ratio (according to Eq. (8)) and recovery ratio (according to Eq. (9)) for Cl− ions decreased when the acid and base concentration increased, as shown in Table 3 . In test ED3 and ED5, which started with 0.4 M HCl, the separation ratio of Cl− ions was 59.16 % and 60.22 %, respectively, compared to 71.15 % and 63.11 % in ED2 and ED4, which started with 0.2 M HCl. Similarly, the recovery ratio for Cl− ions was also the highest in ED2 (68.21 %), while it decreased to 55.87 % in ED5. These results suggest that higher acid concentration increases ionic competition and reduce the driving force for Cl− transport, limiting the net recovery of Cl− ions [14].

Regarding the Li+ ions, it was observed that separation ratio and recovery ratio were not affected by the initial concentration of LiOH. In all cases, the separation ratio was between 67 and 73 % and the recovery ratio was in the range of 63–69 %. These differences can be explained by the transport numbers of the ions. While Li+ transport through CEM remains high and stable, Cl− transport through AEM is more sensitive to ionic competition, particularly at higher acid concentrations. This can be attributed to CEMs only allow the passage of cations, and therefore, the only relevant competitor for Li+ is H+. On the contrary, AEMs also allow the partial transport of protons which generates competitive transport between Cl− and H+.

The initial acid and base concentrations have also an impact on the operational parameters of the electrodialysis process, including voltage, specific energy consumption and current efficiency. Fig. S1 presents the voltage profile of the ED cell for experiments ED2-ED5 as a function of time. As expected, the results indicate that the higher initial acid and base concentration lead to the lowest voltage, as observed in ED5. This is attributed to the highest conductivity of the solutions, which depends on the ion concentration. In all cases, the voltage initially started high, primarily due to the inherent resistance of the membranes and solution before the ion migration. As the experiments advanced, the voltage decreased, stabilizing after approximately 30 min. This stabilization is mainly due to the change in the electrolyte concentration, with an increase in the concentrate compartments and a decrease in the feed compartment. However, after 2 h, the voltage began to rise again, which can be attributed to the reduced conductivity of the feed solution as its ion concentration diminished.

Fig. 12 shows the results for the specific energy consumption (SEC) and current efficiency (CE) to produce HCl and LiOH. As shown in Fig. 12a and Fig. 12b, the SEC decreased from 11.1 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 16.9 kWh·kg−1 LiOH in ED3 (0.4 M HCl, 0.3 M LiOH concentration) to 8.32 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 13.7 kWh·kg−1 LiOH in ED4 (0.2 M HCl, 0.5 M LiOH). These results confirm that SEC is directly related to the initial acid concentration and inversely related to the initial base concentration. If compared with literature (Table S2), the SEC values obtained in this study are within the same range as those reported for BMED (6–10 kWh·kg−1) [14,15] and represent an improvement over conventional ED (23 kWh·kg−1) [13]. Although the SEC for LiOH exceeds that of the most optimized BMED configuration, the proposed four compartment EED allows the simultaneous generation of HCl and LiOH directly from LiCl, using only standard ion-exchange membranes.

Regarding CE, values were relatively high in all tests. For HCl, CE decreased from 81.64 % in ED2 to 65.31 % in ED5 when the initial acid and base concentration increased. In contrast, LiOH reached its maximum CE of 85.5 % in ED5. Although higher initial and base concentration reduces the voltage, it also results in a lower Cl− separation and recovery ratio, which could explain the increase in the SEC when the initial HCl is higher. Moreover, during the ED process, higher acid concentration could hinder the ionic transportation, decreasing the CE [16]. These results indicate that a lower initial acid concentration combined with a higher initial base concentration offers the best overall performance in electrodialysis. This configuration enhances current efficiency and reduces energy consumption while maintaining effective ion separation and recovery ratio.

Experimental and simulation results for the five experiments were analyzed to assess the concentration profiles of HCl, LiCl, and LiOH and the ion recovery ratios for Li+ and Cl−. These data are presented in Figs. S2 and S3 (Supplementary Material), showing the concentration profiles and ion recovery ratios, respectively, allowing a clear comparison between experimental measurements and model simulations. The simulation parameters, listed in Table 4 , provided the best fit to the experimental results.

As can be observed from Fig. S2 and Fig. S3, the simulation results (solid lines) match well with the experimental data (marks), under different experimental conditions, using the same model parameters for all simulations. Fig. 13 shows a comparison between the final experimental and simulation results for key process variables: final concentration (Fig. 13a), ion recovery (Fig. 13b) and specific energy consumption (Fig. 13c). In the three cases, the x-axis represents the experimental results while the y-axis represents the results obtained with the proposed model. As can be observed, the model predicts accurate experimental results, with a R2 higher than 0.99. These results highlight the reliability of the developed model and its potential as a powerful tool to optimize the process, reducing the need for extensive experimental testing and enabling more efficient system design and operation. It should also be noted that the validation presented here corresponds to short-term experiments (3 h), which are sufficient to complete the desalination under the selected conditions. However, extrapolating the model to long-term operation will require further studies on membrane stability, fouling, and scaling, as these factors may significantly affect performance over extended timescales.

In addition to the technical evaluation, a simple cost estimation was carried out to provide a preliminary economic perspective. Considering an average electricity price of 0.15 €·kWh−1 [34], the energy cost derived from the optimized SEC values corresponds to approximately 1.25 €·kg−1 HCl and 2.05 €·kg−1 LiOH. When compared with market prices, 0.20–0.25 €·kg−1 for HCl (35 %) and 15–20 €·kg−1 for LiOH [35], the energy cost contribution is minimal for LiOH and comparable to the market value of HCl. However, in the proposed closed-loop operation, regenerated reagents are directly reused in the leaching and precipitation steps, thus reducing external purchasing and waste treatment costs. This preliminary analysis supports the economic potential of the EED process, while a more detailed techno-economic study will be carried out in future works.

This study demonstrates the potential of electro-electrodialysis for the selective recovery of HCl and LiOH from LiCl solutions, supporting its role in sustainable lithium-ion battery recycling. The validated mathematical model accurately described ion transport across membranes and predicted the system's performance under various operational conditions, showing good agreement with experimental data in terms of ion concentration profiles, recovery ratios, and specific energy consumption.

The experimental results confirmed that both energy efficiency and ion recovery are influenced by the initial concentration of acid and base solutions, as well as the electrode distance. The best performance was achieved with an initial concentration of 0.5 M LiOH and 0.2 M HCl, and an electrode distance of 8 cm. Under these conditions, the specific energy consumption was minimized to 8.3 kWh·kg−1 HCl and 13.7 kWh·kg−1 LiOH produced. These findings confirm the feasibility of electrodialysis for producing acid and base from LiCl-rich solutions, enabling reagent regeneration within lithium-ion battery recycling processes. Moreover, the validated model provides a useful tool for process optimization.

Beyond de electrodialysis performance, the results demonstrate the feasibility of integrating this process within a closed-loop hydrometallurgical process. In this system, the regenerated HCl can be reused in the leaching stage, while the regenerated LiOH can be used for the precipitation of the transition metals. This circular approach would reduce the demand for external reagents, decrease waste regeneration and supports the implementation of sustainable battery recycling processes. A preliminary cost estimation confirmed that the energy demand represents only a minor fraction of the market value of LiOH and is comparable to that of HCl, reinforcing the economic feasibility of the proposed EED process within a closed-loop recycling scheme. Future work will focus on testing the system with real hydrometallurgical leachates and extended operation tests to assess membrane performance and scalability.

Maria del Mar Cerrillo-Gonzalez: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Raffaele Casaburo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Giuseppina Luciani: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Juan Manuel Paz-Garcia: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Jose Miguel Rodriguez-Maroto: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Maria Villen-Guzman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
